era_context: The late 14th century was marked by major geopolitical shifts: the Ottoman Empire was expanding aggressively in the Balkans, the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia and the Middle East, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating Han Chinese rule, and Western European kingdoms were engaged in dynastic conflicts. The Western Schism in the Catholic Church (1378–1417) was creating religious and political instability. This was an era of significant military innovation, early maritime exploration, and the emergence of early modern state structures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Ottoman campaigns in the Balkans under Bayezid I
events.description: Bayezid I continued Ottoman military campaigns in the Balkans, consolidating Ottoman control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. These campaigns were part of the broader Ottoman expansion that would culminate in the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396. The campaigns involved both direct military conquest and diplomatic pressure on local rulers to accept Ottoman suzerainty. The period saw the Ottoman Empire transition from a regional Anatolian power to a major Balkan force.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman military activity in the Balkans during this period is well-attested in general. Specific events of 1393 are less precisely documented than some other years.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion documented from 1389 onward

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's continued consolidation of Ming rule
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor, founder of the Ming Dynasty, continued consolidating Han Chinese rule over China in 1393. The period saw the establishment of Ming administrative structures, the suppression of remaining Yuan loyalists, and the beginning of major construction projects. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule and his efforts to centralize power. His reign laid the groundwork for the Ming Dynasty's later cultural and maritime achievements.
events.certainty_note: Ming consolidation under the Hongwu Emperor in the 1390s is well-attested in general. Specific events of 1393 are less precisely documented.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty consolidation documented from 1368 onward

events.title: Timur's military campaigns and empire consolidation
events.region: Central Asia and Middle East (Timurid Empire)
events.description: Timur continued his military campaigns and consolidation of his vast empire in 1393. His dominance stretched from the Mediterranean to China, and he was organizing administrative structures to maintain control over these territories. Timur's court at Samarkand was becoming a major center of Islamic culture and learning. His military campaigns and administrative innovations shaped the geopolitical landscape of Central Asia, the Middle East, and parts of South Asia.
key_figures: Timur (Tamerlane, Timurid Empire founder and ruler)

sources.name: Persian chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama)

events.certainty_note: Timur's political and military dominance in 1393 is well-attested. Specific events of this year are less precisely documented than some other years.
cross_references: Part of Timurid expansion documented from 1370s onward

events.title: Richard II's consolidation of power in England
events.description: King Richard II of England was consolidating his power in 1393, following his earlier conflicts with the nobility. The period saw Richard attempting to establish a more centralized and authoritarian form of rule, which would eventually lead to conflicts with the English magnates. Richard's policies and personality would contribute to the political tensions that would culminate in his deposition in 1399. The period reflects the broader late medieval trend toward stronger centralized monarchy, though Richard's approach was more confrontational than that of his contemporaries.
events.certainty_note: Richard II's political activities in 1393 are documented in English sources, though specific events of this year are less precisely dated than some other years.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Richard II's consolidation of power as straightforward 'centralization' obscures the complex factional politics of his court. Some historians argue that Richard's actions were defensive responses to magnate power rather than aggressive attempts at tyranny. Additionally, the sources on Richard II are heavily influenced by the perspective of his opponents, who later justified his deposition through hostile chronicles.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1393 is moderate, with relatively strong coverage of Ottoman, Ming, and Western European political history. Ottoman and English sources are abundant, though they reflect court biases. Chinese sources on Ming consolidation are less well-integrated into Western historiography. Persian and Central Asian sources on Timur are available but often reflect the perspective of the conqueror. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific histories remain largely absent from surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman campaigns in 1393 set the stage for the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396
Hongwu Emperor's consolidation in 1393 set the stage for the succession crisis after his death in 1398
Richard II's consolidation of power in 1393 contributed to the political tensions that led to his deposition in 1399
